RlnOfActyToAccomOfExmptPrpsGrp.RelationshipStatementTxt: THE GETTY PUT ON NUMEROUS PUBLIC PERFORMANCES INCLUDING "SELECTED SHORTS", A SERIES OF READINGS OF CLASSIC AND CONTEMPORARY SHORT STORIES AS WELL AS THEATER AND DANCE PERFORMANCES DESIGNED TO ATTRACT AN AUDIENCE THAT MIGHT NOT OTHERWISE VISIT THE MUSEUM. EACH FALL, VILLA OUTDOOR THEATER SERIES FEATURES A CLASSICAL TRAGEDY OR COMEDY AT THE GETTY VILLA THEATER, AN OUTDOOR THEATER BASED ON ANCIENT PROTOTYPES. IN ADDITION, THE GETTY PUTS ON THE VILLA THEATER LAB SERIES, WHICH FEATURES REINTERPRETATIONS OF CLASSICAL THEATER.
THE J. PAUL GETTY MUSEUM PROVIDES A VARIETY OF PROFESSIONAL DEVELOPMENT WORKSHOPS AND TEACHER COLLABORATION WORKSHOPS TO HELP EDUCATORS AND LEADERS INTEGRATE ART EDUCATION INTO THEIR ACTIVITIES.

ROYALTIES ARE RECEIVED FROM THREE SOURCES: 1) ROYALTIES ARE RECEIVED FROM PROQUEST, THE INFORMATION COMPANY THAT HAS CONTRACTED WITH THE GRI TO PROVIDE ACCESS TO THE LEGACY DATA FROM THE FORMER GRI PROJECT, THE INTERNATIONAL BIBLIOGRAPHY OF ART, WHICH PROVIDED COMPREHENSIVE ENGLISH-LANGUAGE INDEXING AND ABSTRACTING FOR ARTICLES, BOOKS AND CATALOGS ON WESTERN ART. (2) WORKING BOTH INDEPENDENTLY AND WITH THE COPYRIGHT CLEARANCE CENTER, GRI PUBLICATIONS LICENSES ITS PREVIOUSLY PUBLISHED CONTENTS TO UNIVERSITIES FOR USE IN COURSE PACKETS AND TO PUBLISHERS IN THE UNITED STATES AND ABROAD WHO WANT TO REPRINT GRI SCHOLARSHIP. (3) THE GETTY RECEIVED ROYALTIES FROM ADA LOUISE HUXTABLE'S PUBLICATIONS.

THE GETTY HOSTS EDUCATIONAL EVENTS FOR CERTAIN LOCAL, NATIONAL AND INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS OPERATING IN THE FIELDS RELATING TO THE GETTY'S MISSION IN ORDER TO FURTHER THE GETTY'S OWN PRIORITIES AND AS A SERVICE TO ORGANIZATIONS WITH COMMON VALUES AND INTERESTS OR WITH OBJECTIVES THAT MEET THE PUBLIC NEEDS. UPON REQUEST, THE MUSEUM AND THE RESEARCH INSTITUTE MAKE AVAILABLE PHOTOGRAPHS AND SLIDES OF WORKS OF ART IN ITS COLLECTIONS. THESE PHOTOGRAPHS ARE PROVIDED FREE OF CHARGE TO SCHOLARS AND ARTS INSTITUTIONS. A NOMINAL CHARGE IS MADE FOR OTHER REQUESTS TO OFFSET PRODUCTION COSTS. THE MUSEUM ALSO RECEIVED REIMBURSEMENTS FOR COSTS INCURRED IN CO-ORGANIZING EXHIBITIONS WITH OTHER MUSEUMS. IN ADDITION, THE GETTY PROVIDES CONSERVATION SERVICES ON ART COLLECTIONS FROM OTHER ART INSTITUTIONS.

THE MUSEUM BOOKSTORE MAKES AVAILABLE TO THE PUBLIC A WIDE VARIETY OF GENERAL ART BOOKS AS WELL AS PUBLICATIONS, PRINTS AND POSTCARDS SPECIFIC TO THE MUSEUM'S COLLECTIONS. ADDITIONALLY, SCHOLARLY PUBLICATIONS PRODUCED BY J. PAUL GETTY TRUST PUBLICATIONS, THE RESEARCH INSTITUTE AND THE CONSERVATION INSTITUTE ARE AVAILABLE FROM THE BOOKSTORE.

Paul Getty Trust provides an Investment Incentive and Bonus Plan to certain employees. Accruals under this plan are reported as deferred compensation on Form 990-PF, Part VIII, Column (d). When amounts under this plan are no longer subject to a substantial risk of forfeiture, these amounts will be reported again as compensation of Part VIII, Column (c). Expenditure Responsibility Grants PART VII-B, QUESTION 5(C) IN ACCORDANCE WITH IRS REGULATION 53.4945-5(D)(2), THE FOLLOWING INFORMATION IS PROVIDED REGARDING GRANTS MADE OR OUTSTANDING DURING THE YEAR TO ORGANIZATIONS NOT QUALIFYING UNDER IRC 509(A)(1), 509(A)(2), 509(A)(3) OR 4940(D)(2). FOR EACH EXPENDITURE RESPONSIBILITY GRANT, THE (1) NAME AND ADDRESS OF THE GRANTEE, (2) DATE AND AMOUNT OF THE GRANT, (3) PURPOSE OF THE GRANT, (4) AMOUNTS EXPENDED BY THE GRANTEE (BASED ON THE MOST RECENT REPORT RECEIVED FROM THE GRANTEE), (5) DATES OF ANY REPORTS RECEIVED FROM THE GRANTEE, (6) DATE AND RESULTS OF ANY VERIFICATION OF THE GRANTEE'S REPORTS UNDERTAKEN BY OR AT THE DIRECTION OF THE J. PAUL GETTY TRUST AND (7) WHETHER, TO THE KNOWLEDGE OF THE J. PAUL GETTY TRUST, IF THE GRANTEE HAS DIVERTED ANY FUNDS FROM THE PURPOSE OF THE GRANT. NOTE, TO THE BEST OF OUR KNOWLEDGE AND BELIEF, THERE WERE NO DIVERSIONS OF GRANT FUNDS BY ANY GRANTEE for ANY USE NOT IN FURTHERANCE OF A PURPOSE SPECIFIED IN THE GRANT. ALSO NOTE THAT FOR GRANTS APPROVED OR PAID IN THE CURRENT TAX YEAR, INFORMATION REGARDING THE AMOUNTS EXPENDED BY THE GRANTEE AND THE DATES OF ANY REPORTS RECEIVED FROM THE GRANTEE IS NOT AVAILABLE AT THE TIME OF THE FILING OF THIS RETURN. SEE ATTACHMENT 22 FOR fURTHER DETAILS.

GeneralExplanationGrp.IdentifierTxt: Part IX-A Summary of Direct Charitable Activities Line 1
GeneralExplanationGrp.FormAndLineReferenceDesc: Part IX-A Summary of Direct Charitable Activities Line 1
GeneralExplanationGrp.MediumExplanationTxt: THE J. PAUL GETTY MUSEUM AT THE GETTY CENTER AND THE J. PAUL GETTY MUSEUM AT THE GETTY VILLA The J. Paul Getty Museum collects Greek and Roman antiquities, European paintings, drawings, manuscripts, sculpture and decorative arts to 1900, as well as photographs from around the world to the present day. The Museum's mission is to display and interpret its collections, and present important loan exhibitions and publications for the enjoyment and education of visitors locally and internationally. This is supported by an active program of research, conservation, and public programs that seek to deepen our knowledge of and connection to works of art. The Museum operates two sites in Los Angeles - one at the Getty Center; the second at the Getty Villa. Due to the COVID-19 Global Pandemic, effective Saturday, March 14, 2020, the Getty Center and Getty Villa locations were closed to the public until further notice. All FY20 data provided in this report occurred prior closing (July 1, 2019-March 13, 2020). J. Paul Getty Museum, Statistical Highlights, FY20 The Getty Center 968,266 people visited the Museum at the Getty Center free of charge during FY20. In addition to presenting works of art from the permanent collection and 28 temporary exhibitions, the Museum provided a wide variety of free interpretive materials, programs, and services on a daily basis. These included gallery, architecture, and garden tours along with usage of interactive and educational spaces which served families with children. There were 14 free family art drop-in workshops with 5,149 attendees in which the Getty provided all necessary materials. The school program served approximately 54,697 students and teachers during FY20 and spent $391,833 on bus transportation for Title 1 Schools. The Getty Museum provided, free-of-charge lectures, symposia, artist-at-work demonstrations, and drawing-based programs with 6,129 attendees. 11,623 attendees from colleges and universities (non-profit organizations) took advantage of our self-guided tours and education resources. 40,358 general public visitors were provided guided gallery tours. The Getty Villa 305,270 people visited the Museum at the Getty Villa free of charge during FY20. In addition to presenting works of art from the permanent collection and 3 temporary exhibitions, the Museum provided a wide variety of free interpretive materials, programs, and services on a daily basis. These included gallery, architecture, garden, and orientation tours along with interactive and educational spaces which served families with children. There were 9 free family art drop-in workshops with 2,719 attendees in which the Getty provided all necessary materials. The Villa school program served approximately 32,460 students and teachers and spent $187,375 on bus transportation for Title 1 Schools. The Getty Villa provided, free-of-charge lectures, symposia, artist-at-work demonstrations, and drawing-based programs with 4,454 attendees. 5,383 attendees from colleges and universities (non-profit organizations) took advantage of our self-guided tours and education resources. Villa visitors also attended 12,135 drop-in gallery tours, including collection highlights tours, spotlight talks, special exhibition tours, and curator led gallery talks. J. Paul Getty Museum, Highlights of Accomplishments, FY20 Exhibitions Manet and Modern Beauty douard Manet was a provocateur and a dandy, the Impressionist generation's great painter of modern Paris. This first-ever exhibition to explore the last years of Manet's short life and career revealed a fresh and surprisingly intimate aspect of this celebrated artist's work. Stylish portraits, luscious still lifes, delicate pastels and watercolors, and vivid caf and garden scenes convey Manet's elegant social world and reveal his growing fascination with fashion, flowers, and modern femininity, as embodied in the parisienne. This exhibition was co-organized by the J. Paul Getty Museum and the Art Institute of Chicago. Michelangelo: Mind of a Master This exhibition explored the full range of Michelangelo's work as a painter, sculptor, and architect through more than two dozen of his extraordinary drawings, including designs for celebrated projects such as the Sistine Chapel ceiling, the Medici Chapel tombs, and The Last Judgment. These studies and sketches enable us to witness Michelangelo at work, and to experience firsthand his boundless creativity and his pioneering representation of the human form. Buried by Vesuvius: Treasures from the Villa dei Papiri The Getty Villa is modeled on the Villa dei Papiri at Herculaneum. Buried by the eruption of Mount Vesuvius in AD 79, the ancient villa was rediscovered and explored by subterranean tunnels in the 1750s and '60s and was partially re-excavated in the 1990s and early 2000s. It has yielded colorful marble and mosaic floors, frescoed walls, a large collection of bronze and marble statuary, and a unique library of more than a thousand papyrus scrolls. This exhibition presented many of the most spectacular finds and examines attempts to unroll and decipher the carbonized papyri. Gordon Parks: The Flvio Story While on assignment to document poverty in Brazil for Life magazine, African American photographer Gordon Parks encountered one of the most important subjects of his career: Flvio da Silva. Parks featured the resourceful, ailing boy, who lived with his family in one of Rio's working-class neighborhoods known as favelas, in his 1961 photo essay "Freedom's Fearful Foe: Poverty." This exhibition explored the celebrated photo essay, tracing the extraordinary chain of events it triggered and Parks's representation of Flvio over several decades. Conservation Drunken Satyr One of a handful of large-scale bronze sculptures to survive from the ancient classical world, this statue was brought to the Museum for study and conservation treatment, and was a centerpiece of the exhibition Buried by Vesuvius: Treasures from the Villa dei Papiri (June 26-October 27, 2019). It depicts a drunken satyr (satiro ebbro)-a follower of the wine god Dionysos in Greek and Roman mythology, half man, half goat-and once formed part of the sculpture collection of the Villa dei Papiri (Villa of the Papyri) in Herculaneum. The bronze left Italy for the first time since it was made over 2,000 years ago, on rare loan from the National Archaeological Museum of Naples. Public Programs Outdoor Classical Theater Production of The Heal A bold new version of Sophocles's timeless tale of hurting-and healing, co-produced by Maryland's Round House Theatre. Director/adaptor Aaron Posner created an irreverent, spiritual, musical exploration about the wounds we carry, the ones we cause, and the redeeming power of human connection. Presented in the Getty Villa's Barbara and Lawrence Fleischman Theater, an outdoor venue modeled after ancient Greek and Roman theaters. New Acquisitions Bronzino The Getty Museum acquired the exquisitely preserved painting Virgin and Child with Saint Elizabeth and Saint John the Baptist (about 1540-45) by Agnolo Bronzino, Florence's leading painter in the mid-16th century. The acquisition demonstrates Bronzino's sought-after skill in adjusting his paintings' luminosities to resemble different times of day. Digital Initiatives Google Arts & Culture Nearly 20 exhibition presentations were produced for Google Arts & Culture, which delivers a significantly more robust online presence. These new presentations take advantage of Google's high traffic and technology to provide up-close views of objects in the collection, giving visitors a new way to explore and engage with artworks in a way that is not possible in the galleries.

GeneralExplanationGrp.IdentifierTxt: Part IX-A Summary of Direct Charitable Activities Line 2
GeneralExplanationGrp.FormAndLineReferenceDesc: Part IX-A Summary of Direct Charitable Activities Line 2
GeneralExplanationGrp.MediumExplanationTxt: THE GETTY RESEARCH INSTITUTE ("GRI") Founded in 1983 as the research program of the J. Paul Getty Trust, the Getty Research Institute (GRI) has been dedicated to advancing understanding of the arts and humanities for more than 35 years. The GRI's chief purpose is to contribute to Getty's overarching mission to advance and share the world's visual art and cultural heritage for the benefit of all. The GRI is, in particular, driven by three major directives: to promote discovery of materials related to art and art history; to create new knowledge about visual culture and the humanities; and to advance scholarly and public understanding of such knowledge through the dissemination of research. The GRI provides intellectual leadership through its research, exhibition, and publication programs, and supports scholarship by hosting fellowships and grants, digital resources, and the Getty Library, one of the largest art history libraries and archives in the world. The Getty Library's general library collections (secondary sources) include almost 1.5 million volumes of books, periodicals, and auction catalogues encompassing the history of Western art and related fields in the humanities. The Getty Library's special collections include rare books, artists' journals, sketchbooks, architectural drawings and models, photographs, and archival materials. The Getty Library provided a variety of services to external users including 19,561 in-person library visits, classes, and seminars; 35,106 book loans; and 13,388 reference requests fulfilled from requesters around the world. It also loaned 1081 original items for public exhibition to outside institutions. The Getty Research Portal has 38 contributing institutions, and now offers global and open access to nearly 175,000 digitized art history volumes. In the past year, more than 215,500 searches were performed in the Portal. The Getty Research Institute awarded a total of 93 grants, including (51)* grants to Scholar and Fellow participants in the scholar year, (34) Library Research Grants, and (8) Graduate Interns. *Footnote: 51 includes GRI Scholars & Fellows, JPGM Scholars, and GCI Scholars (no guest researchers). These grants were in the form of stipend payments. Johnson Publishing Company Acquisition In collaboration with the Ford Foundation, MacArthur Foundation, Mellon Foundation, and Smithsonian, Getty acquired the photographic archive of the Johnson Publishing Company, publisher of magazines Ebony, Jet, and other iconic 20th-century publications. http://blogs.getty.edu/iris/consortium-forms-advisory-council-announces- plans-to-preserve-historic-ebony-and-jet-photographic-archive/ Bauhaus Exhibitions Two exhibitions, one on-site and another online, explored the Bauhaus' unique impact on arts-related teaching and learning through rare archival materials at the Getty Research Institute, in honor of the Bauhaus' centennial. https://www.getty.edu/research/exhibitions_events/exhibitions/bauhaus/ index.html Celebrating 25 years of the Library Research Grant Program Since its launch in 1995, the Getty Research Institute has hosted over 650 students and researchers from around the world through its Library Research Grant Program, which awards small grants for short-term research in the Getty Library, including Special Collections. https://www.getty.edu/foundation/initiatives/residential/library_research _grants.html Art & Ecology Scholar Year Goes Remote With the onset of COVID-19, Getty Scholars turned their year into a virtual experience, staying "safer at home," both in Los Angeles and around the world, to explore themes of art, the environment, and ecological change. https://www.getty.edu/research/scholars/years/2019_2020.html Radical Women Podcast Focusing on the lives and careers of six women artists, this award-winning podcast combines oral histories held in the Getty Research Institute's collections with insights from contemporary artists and historians to explore the impact of feminism on twentieth-century art and art history. https://www.getty.edu/recordingartists/ International Symposia on Mesoamerican cultural heritage The Getty Research Institute hosted two international symposia on the art and history of Pre-Hispanic and colonial Mexico, advancing scholarship and awareness of indigenous Latin American cultural heritage. https://www.getty.edu/visit/cal/events/ev_2728.html https://www.getty.edu/visit/cal/events/ev_2774.html https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLdcw4RhcVX8vuhiyafe3gu3206GRsf_bc Acquisition of the Archive of Architect Paul R. Williams The Getty Research Institute and USC School of Architecture jointly acquired the archive of renowned architect Paul Revere Williams in an effort to make the archive accessible to students and other researchers. http://blogs.getty.edu/iris/the-legacy-of-paul-revere-williams/

GeneralExplanationGrp.IdentifierTxt: Part IX-A Summary of Direct Charitable Activities Line 3
GeneralExplanationGrp.FormAndLineReferenceDesc: Part IX-A Summary of Direct Charitable Activities Line 3
GeneralExplanationGrp.MediumExplanationTxt: The Getty Conservation Institute ("GCI") The Getty Conservation Institute (GCI) is an operating program of the J. Paul Getty Trust. The GCI works internationally to advance conservation practice in the visual arts-broadly interpreted to include objects, collections, architecture, and sites. The Institute serves the conservation community through scientific research, education and training, field projects, and the dissemination of information. In all its endeavors, the GCI creates and delivers knowledge that contributes to the conservation of the world's cultural heritage. The GCI's free online comprehensive research database of abstracts and key resource for the professionals in the field of conservation, AATA Online, reviewed, abstracted, and indexed nearly 3,000 articles and papers from journals and conference proceedings representing the field's core literature. In addition to this important ongoing work, AATA launched a new and enhanced search interface. During the 2020 fiscal year, AATA Online logged over 35,000 search sessions by users across the globe. The GCI's Information Center supported the mission of the Institute by providing expertise and support to the work of conservation staff throughout the Getty and conservation professionals worldwide. The center responded to over 3,200 requests from staff, scholars, interns, and members of the interested public; acquired over 1,300 new titles for the Conservation Collection in the Research Library at the Getty Research Institute which increased the collection to more than 56,000 titles. The Getty Conservation Institute advanced its residency program by hosting; a two-year postdoctoral fellow in the Science department working with the GCI Science's 'Treatment Studies' research area; three, three-year fellowships for conservators interested in preventive conservation and the management of museum environments; four conservation guest scholars conducting research on their own topics including the preservation of industrial architecture, conservation as data science through machine learning, the conservation of African Modernist buildings and structures, and the future of heritage conservation in a world of increasing challenges. The GCI's YouTube channel made available over 200 videos on a variety of GCI activities including: GCI projects, lectures, symposia and conferences for conservation professionals around the world. During the 2020 fiscal year, GCI videos have received over 260,000 views from around the world for a total of 12,000 hours watched. The GCI section of the Getty website (getty.edu/conservation/) is regularly updated throughout the year with content and enhancements to provide conservation professionals with access to knowledge, resources, and opportunities. Among the additions to the website in FY20 were links to professional opportunities including the Conservation Guest Scholar Program, Graduate Internship Program, Postdoctoral Fellowship in Conservation Science, new Library Research Grants to conduct research using the Conservation Collection. Two issues of Conservation Perspectives, The GCI Newsletter were distributed free of charge around the world to over 8,000 international professionals in conservation and related fields and members of the public concerned about conservation. The GCI Bulletin, a free bimonthly e-bulletin providing up-to-date information on the activities of the Getty Conservation Institute, was distributed free of charge to professional colleagues around the world. In FY20, the number of professional conservation-related publications offered free of charge increased to 182, with seven new titles added in FY20: Proyecto de Estabilizacin Sismorresistente: Ensayo de materiales y components de la construccin de edificios histricos de adobe de Per; Cleaning of Wooden Gilded Surfaces; The Buildings of Louis Kahn: Conserving Exterior Wood; Proyecto de Estabilizacin Sismorresistente: Recomendaciones para el modelado avanzado de sitios histricos de tierra; Handheld XRF in Cultural Heritage: A Practical Workbook for Conservators; Acoustic Emission Monitoring for Cultural Heritage; Proyecto de Estabilizacin Sismorresistente: Modelado de edificios tipo. The Getty Conservation Institute's Science department led 26 tours of our facilities to conservation professionals or groups, local colleges and universities, local high school groups, and a variety of international professional organizations. As part of the GCI's communication and dissemination program strategy, the GCI staffs and supports a booth at professional conferences with the objective of disseminating information about our work, online resources, publications, and professional opportunities to our core audience of cultural heritage conservation professionals and those in allied fields. In FY20, the GCI booth, featuring the Conserving Modern Architecture Initiative, was present at the APT Conference. The GCI was represented at the AIC 48th Annual Virtual Meeting through four Meet and Greet presentations. Bagan Project Launched In November 2019 the Getty Conservation Institute and Myanmar's Department of Archaeology and National Museum launched a multiyear collaborative project to conserve the ancient site of Bagan, a complex of over 3,500 temples, pagodas, and monasteries. The project is addressing a variety of conservation issues across the vast Bagan Archaeological Zone, and includes developing methods to repair buildings damaged by earthquakes and improve resistance to future seismic events; identifying means of conserving the site's decorative elements; developing conservation principles and approaches to manage the range of conservation challenges across the site; and training local professionals to sustain conservation efforts. https://blogs.getty.edu/iris/bagan-getty-partners-conserve-ancient-site/ Project in Chandigarh In January 2020 the Getty Conservation Institute began work with colleagues at the Government Museum and Art Gallery in Chandigarh, India, one of only three museum buildings designed by the great modernist architect Charles-douard Jeanneret, better known as Le Corbusier. The Getty team collaborated with Indian colleagues on a risk assessment of the museum's collection-regarded as one of the most significant in India-and also initiated a yearlong campaign to monitor and better understand the environment of the museum and its impact on both the building and the collection. Paphos Conservation and Management Plan During FY20 the Getty Conservation Institute and the Department of Antiquities (DoA) in Cyprus advanced their collaboration to develop a conservation and management plan for Nea Paphos, one of the richest archaeological sites of mosaic pavements in the eastern Mediterranean region, with significant remains from the Hellenistic, Roman, early Christian, and Byzantine periods, as well as Frankish and Ottoman monuments. Mapping the site, one of the first steps in the creation of the plan, has been a priority, and with the final development of the GIS and production of maps of the site-including topographical maps, digital surface models, and plans of all buildings and underground features-Nea Paphos has now been fully mapped. New Interface for AATA Online A new user interface for the Getty Conservation Institute's AATA Online, a major resource for the conservation field, launched in April 2020. This database-which contains citations, abstracts, and indexing terms that conservation and heritage professionals have relied on for years-now provides vastly improved search features and functionality. Notable enhancements include linked author names and indexing terms, additional search result filters, links to full-text articles that have digital object identifiers, and a more intuitive look and feel. AATA Online researchers will also be able to more easily save and share records, generate citations and bibliographies, and export records to reference management programs.

GeneralExplanationGrp.IdentifierTxt: PART IX-A SUMMARY OF DIRECT CHARITABLE ACTIVITIES LINE 4
GeneralExplanationGrp.FormAndLineReferenceDesc: PART IX-A SUMMARY OF DIRECT CHARITABLE ACTIVITIES LINE 4
GeneralExplanationGrp.MediumExplanationTxt: The Getty Foundation FY20 Getty Foundation The Getty Foundation fulfills the philanthropic mission of the Getty Trust by supporting individuals and institutions committed to advancing the greater understanding and preservation of the visual arts in Los Angeles and throughout the world. Through strategic grant initiatives, it strengthens art history as a global discipline, promotes the interdisciplinary practice of conservation, increases access to museum and archival collections, and develops current and future leaders in the visual arts. It carries out its work in collaboration with the other Getty Programs to ensure that they individually and collectively achieve maximum effect. Since its inception, the Foundation has carried out signature grant programs that have made art history more interdisciplinary and international; created models for the practice of conservation emphasizing the importance of planning and training; increased access to museum and archival collections, most recently in digital form; and nurtured a generation of new leaders in the visual arts. To date, the Foundation has developed, assessed, awarded, and monitored over 8,000 grants in more than 180 countries. The Foundation awarded 141 "core" grants in FY20 as well as 35 grants for Graduate Internships and 15 grants for Getty Marrow Undergraduate Internships. In addition, the Foundation processed and awarded 205 matching gift grants and 106 grants on behalf of Getty's operating programs (the Getty Research Institute, the J. Paul Getty Museum, and the Getty Conservation Institute) for scholars to complete research at Getty and special projects. COVID-19 Relief Fund Responds to Crisis in the Arts Sector In response to the coronavirus pandemic, Getty established a special fund administered in partnership with the California Community Foundation to provide relief and recovery grants to struggling local visual arts organizations and artists. The fund completed its first grantmaking phase in FY20 by providing emergency relief to 80 institutions and 400 individual artists. https://www.getty.edu/about/whatwedo/getty_magazine/gettymag_ summer2020.pdf Getty Marrow Interns Continue During Work From Home For nearly 30 years, the Foundation has supported paid summers internships in the visual arts for students from diverse cultural backgrounds that have been underrepresented in the staff of Los Angeles' museums and arts organizations. Despite the ongoing closures of these institutions due to COVID-19, Getty maintained its commitment to the program and worked with host organizations to adapt its structure and offer internships remotely for 2020. Digital Art History Grants Tackle Image Analysis Computers can be "trained" to process or analyze massive sets of digital image files in record time, and a pair of Foundation grants to Cornell University and the University of California, Davis are enabling researchers to develop new platforms that apply this technology to the field of art history. https://www.getty.edu/foundation/initiatives/current/dah/dah_grants_ awarded.html#IMAGE%20ANALYSIS%20PROJECTS Renewal of Getty/ACLS Postdoctoral Fellowships A major grant awarded to the American Council of Learned Societies will fund another three years of the Getty/ACLS Postdoctoral Fellowship program. These non-residential research fellowships move the discipline of art history forward by supporting new research of emerging scholars working worldwide on topics as diverse as Inca ceremonial weavings to sacred healing arts in the Black Atlantic. http://blogs.getty.edu/iris/a-year-of-freedom-for-scholars-yields-new- knowledge-in-art-history/ Conserving Canvas Grants Increase Technical Expertise in Latin America Conserving Canvas is a grant initiative focused on expanding conservators' knowledge and skills for the structural care of paintings made on fabric supports. In FY20, the initiative had its first grants to train conservators in Latin America through the treatment of modern abstractions painted by artists Frank Stella and Maria Luisa Pacheco. https://www.getty.edu/about/whatwedo/getty_magazine/gettymag_win2020.pdf Keeping It Modern Expands to New Countries Worldwide Modern architecture is found throughout the world, but its experimental materials and techniques mean this significant heritage is decaying at an alarming rate. Keeping It Modern is a global initiative to conserve twentieth century buildings across the globe, and the penultimate round of grants announced in FY20 expanded the initiative to include model projects for the preservation of modern architecture in Argentina, Bulgaria, Lithuania, Mozambique, and Uganda. http://blogs.getty.edu/iris/getty-grants-modernist-architecture-around- the-world/ New Revelations in the Graphic Arts for Paper Project Fellows The Paper Project provides training and professional development for curators of works on paper, some of the most voluminous collections in many museums that demand specialized understanding to be managed properly. New curatorial fellowships throughout Europe and North America funded by Getty grants are allowing early career scholars to immerse themselves in the cataloguing and study of leading museum collections and make new research discoveries. http://blogs.getty.edu/iris/paper-people-reflections-from-emerging- curators-of-prints-and-drawings/

GeneralExplanationGrp.IdentifierTxt: Part XV, Line 2 Information Regarding Grant Programs
GeneralExplanationGrp.FormAndLineReferenceDesc: Part XV, Line 2 Information Regarding Grant Programs
GeneralExplanationGrp.MediumExplanationTxt: The Getty Foundation The Getty Foundation fulfills the philanthropic mission of the J. Paul Getty Trust by supporting individuals and institutions committed to advancing the understanding and preservation of the visual arts locally and throughout the world. Through strategic grants and programs, the Foundation strengthens art history as a global discipline, promotes the interdisciplinary practice of conservation, increases access to museum and archival collections, and develops current and future leaders in the visual arts. The Foundation carries out its work in collaboration with the J. Paul Getty Museum, the Getty Research Institute, and the Getty Conservation Institute to ensure that the Getty programs achieve maximum impact. The majority of the Getty Foundation's grants for institutions and organizations are awarded through special initiatives focused on a specific issue or region. In a number of cases, applications are by invitation rather than through open competition. Additional information, as well as grants awarded reports, is available online and from the Getty Foundation office. Inquiries and requests for additional information should be sent to: The Getty Foundation 1200 Getty Center Drive, Suite 800 Los Angeles, CA 90049-1685 Phone: 310 440-7320 Fax : 310 440-7703 Email: gettyfoundation@getty.edu Web : http://www.getty.edu/foundation Getty Residential Grants* The J. Paul Getty Trust makes grants to scholars, conservation professionals, and students through various types of its residential grants, as described below. Grantees pursue their work at the Getty Center or the Getty Villa. Residential Research Grants The Getty's residential research grants enable scholars and other specialists to pursue independent research, collaborate with their colleagues, and work on special scholarly projects at the Getty Center or the Getty Villa. Grants related to specific themes are offered to scholars at various levels by the Getty Research Institute. The Research Institute also provides short-term library research grants to scholars for projects that will benefit from research in the collections housed in the Getty Library. In addition, the Getty Conservation Institute offers support for professionals and scholars in conservation and allied fields to pursue independent research at the Getty Center or the Getty Villa. Awards are made through a competitive online application process, and grant periods vary from one month to two years. Select scholars who are uniquely qualified to add their knowledge and expertise to the annual theme or specialized topic may be invited to enhance a cohort. The deadline for residential research grants varies between the Getty Research Institute and the Getty Conservation Institute from October 1 to November 1 of each year, respectively. Applications are received online and additional information is available through the website and from: Attn: Getty Scholar Grants The Getty Foundation 1200 Getty Center Drive, Suite 800 Los Angeles, CA 90049-1685 Phone: (310) 440-7374 Email: researchgrants@getty.edu Web : www.getty.edu/foundation/funding/ residential/getty_scholars.html Graduate Internships Getty Graduate Internships are offered in the four programs of the J. Paul Getty Trust-the J. Paul Getty Museum, the Getty Research Institute, the Getty Conservation Institute, and the Getty Foundation-to students who intend to pursue careers in fields related to the visual arts. The Graduate Internship Program provides training and work experience in areas such as curatorial, education, conservation, research, information management, public programs, and grant making for eight- or twelve-month periods. The deadline for Graduate Internship applications is November 1 of each year. Applications are received online and additional information is available through the website and from: Attn: Graduate Internship Program The Getty Foundation 1200 Getty Center Drive, Suite 800 Los Angeles, CA 90049-1685 Phone: (310) 440-7320 Email: gradinterns@getty.edu Web: www.getty.edu/foundation/funding/leaders/current/grad_internships.html Getty Marrow Undergraduate Internships at the Getty Undergraduate Internships are offered in various programs and departments of the Getty Trust for ten consecutive weeks during the summer. Internships are intended to increase diversity in the professions related to museums and the visual arts and provide training and work experience in areas such as conservation, library collections, publications, museum education, curatorship, grants administration, public programs, site operations, and information technology. Getty Marrow Undergraduate Internships at the Getty are specifically intended for outstanding students, including but not limited to members of ethnic groups currently underrepresented in museum professions and fields related to the visual arts and humanities and who are currently enrolled undergraduates who reside or attend college in Los Angeles County. The deadline for Getty Marrow Undergraduate Internships at the Getty applications is February 1 of each year. Applications are received online and additional information is available through the website and from: The Getty Foundation 1200 Getty Center Drive, Suite 800 Los Angeles, CA 90049-1685 Phone: (310) 440-7320 Email: summerinterns@getty.edu Web : www.getty.edu/foundation/funding/leaders/current/multicultural_undergrad _internships.html Getty Marrow Undergraduate Internships at L.A. Arts Organizations Grants to Los Angeles-area museums and visual arts organizations are offered in order to fund internships for the next generation of arts professionals. The internships are intended specifically for currently enrolled undergraduates and recent graduates residing or attending college in Los Angeles County, including but not limited to, members of groups currently underrepresented in museums and visual arts organizations. These internships are designed to offer eligible students experience in key areas such as curatorial work, conservation, education, publications, and related programmatic activities. The deadline for Getty Marrow Undergraduate Internships at L.A. organizations applications is February 1 of each year. Applications are received online and additional information is available through the website and from: The Getty Foundation 1200 Getty Center Drive, Suite 800 Los Angeles, CA 90049-1685 Phone: (310) 440-7320 Email: summerinterns@getty.edu Web : www.getty.edu/foundation/funding/leaders/current/multicultural_ undergrad_internships.html J. Paul Getty Museum Guest Scholars The Getty Museum offers three-month residential appointments to invited guest scholars working on projects related to the Museum's collections or those of neighboring institutions. These appointments are selected by invitation only. The deadline for invited applications is November 15. Additional information is available by contacting the office of the J. Paul Getty Museum's Associate Director for Collections at the address below: The J. Paul Getty Museum Associate Director for Collections 1200 Getty Center Drive, Suite 1000 Los Angeles, CA 90049-1687 Phone: (310) 440-7330 Fax : (310) 440-7751 Email: gettymuseum@getty.edu Web : www.getty.edu/museum/about.html Other Grants The J. Paul Getty Trust from time to time makes unsolicited grants to charitable organizations involved in advancing the understanding and preservation of the visual arts. The J. Paul Getty Trust also has programs wherein gifts and grants made by its trustees and employees to qualifying charitable organizations are matched. All grant programs of the J. Paul Getty Trust are administered by the Getty Foundation. Further information about grant initiatives may be found on the Getty Foundation's website: http://www.getty.edu/foundation *Due to the COVID-19 crisis and closure of the Getty sites and facilities beginning in mid-March 2020, residential scholar, fellow, and intern programs were postponed or converted to remote terms with comparable expectations and requirements.
